result:
Maybe it’s nothing in particular, or maybe it’s everything at once.

The light from my window is the color of a peeled tangerine just before noon, the curtains twitching like they’re remembering dreams they never had. I overheard my neighbor laughing this morning—the way it spilled down the stairwell I swear it sounded exactly like warm syrup poured over pancakes. The kind of sound you wish you could bottle for air-mail to sad friends.

I keep thinking about memory as a physical place, as architecture rather than story. Somewhere in the corridors of my skull there’s a room lined with terracotta tiles, where every flip-flop slap from childhood summers has left a faint echo against the walls. The room smells of chlorine and melted red, white, and blue rocket popsicles, the kind that stained tongues like patriotism left out in the sun too long. Each summer, another corner of the room gets repainted by my unlicensed future-self, who carries a rollerbrush heavy with nostalgia and just slightly too much nicotine. He whistles Stranger Things synth lines because the eighties walked right up to the present, tapped it on the shoulder, and said, “Look, Ma, no anachronism.” My future-self wears crocs ironically; I suppose every era is doomed to be swallowed by the irony it creates.

Yesterday I read that the Voyager probes are still sending back whispers from the edge of the Solar System, and I pictured the universe as an old gramophone playing a record that ends in endless hiss. Somasssn 14-li is a star with a song too faint for human ears—but somewhere on TikTok a dog tilts its head the same 15 degrees as Voyager turned its parabolic ear, and maybe that’s close enough. Maybe meaning isn’t hiding in the melody but in listening itself, the patient, dog-like cock of the head toward the unhearable.

I once met a man who tattooed the coordinates of everywhere he was heartbroken in flowchart boxes across his ribs: 36.1699 N, 115.1398 W (Las Vegas), 40.7589 N, 73.9851 W (41st Street subway platform), 60.1699 N, 24.9384 E (a Helsinki bookshop). The arrows between them suggest the next heartbreak will be at 34.0522 N, 118.2437 W—Los Angeles, naturally. The skin has tiny predictive powers, he claimed, minutely more accurate than weather apps. He’s thinking of switching to cartography instead because rivers are less capricious than people.

Sometimes I imagine writing a book that can only be read while walking. Every paragraph so fat with motion that if you stop, the letters liquefy into puddles at your feet. You would need rain boots and good knees to finish that novel. The ending is somewhere near the lighthouse on Point Reyes, but tides keep adjusting the page numbers; the author left instructions to trust the fog.

Other times I think the real project is simpler: catalog every color that has made me close my eyes involuntarily because the beauty was too sharp. #FF4F4F Pink salt lakes seen from a plane, the sudden red of taillights on wet blacktop, the exact green of a subway turnstile swipe-card slot lit from behind by a single LED blooming like a vengeless emerald star. Put them side by side and you have the spectrum that humans never agree on, the private weather that slides under the skin before words have permission.

My grandmother believed that if you broke a plate on New Year’s Day you were letting the year practice its own breakage early. She swept the pieces into a paper bag, tied it with string, and whispered Sofia Loren lines to the broken porcelain. “We must leave crumbs of ourselves in every room, tesoro, otherwise how will the rooms know which ones we loved?” The shards eventually became a mosaic on the path to the lemon trees, little ceramic smiles glinting like unfinished blessings.

On Tuesdays I open a blank document and set the font to the sound of humming. It never appears on the page, but I feel it—like tin shivering across the teeth. In that fizzling silence, sometimes a sentence rises by itself: a needle-shaped lifeboat launched through an ocean of cursor. I don’t push it toward meaning; I let it float. If the sentence is really about how I once blamed entropy for every breakup, well, maybe entropy deserves a defense attorney too.

I want to tell the ulcers in my stomach that they’re working overtime for a storyline that keeps rewriting its ending. I want to tell the downstairs couple fighting in two languages at once that every argument is secretly an exorcism of ghosts neither of them met in this lifetime. I want to order pizza and ask the delivery guy whether he’s ever carried anything hotter than hope across threshold and foyer. Tip him with a post-it note that says, “Your twin somewhere is tracking minute hand shadows across an empty room, wondering why no one knocks anymore. Rest assured, in a parallel kitchen tonight, there is still cheese.”

I’m trying to build a life that feels like a net, but not the kind that traps. More like the hammock strung between the two masts of an invisible sailboat drifting inside a day. A place to lie back with seashell ears and wonder whether the whales remember the version of the planet with the supercontinent yolk still intact. Whether one of them keeps an eye on Pangaea the way you hang onto a photograph when the person in it no longer ages.

We are all surrounded by quiet invitations. A dandelion seed balancing on a dog’s whisker. The sudden geometry of toothpaste splatter on the mirror that looks, if you squint, like the Fibonnaci spiral trying to text you privately. The way someone’s voice cracks on the word “home.” Immense architectures made of momentary yeses, and we are temporary tenants galloping across them in rented daylight.

I think—at least for today—that’s enough.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
